sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: σκύφος τε κισσοῦ παρέθετ ’ εἰς εὖρος τριῶν
sections.translation: and by his side he put a can of ivy-wood , whose breadth was three cubits and its depth four maybe ; next he set his brazen pot a-boiling on the fire , [*] spits too he set beside him , fashioned of the branches of thorn , their points hardened in the fire and the rest of them trimmed with the hatchet ,

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: Αἰτναῖά τε σφαγεῖα πελέκεων γνάθοις .
sections.translation: and the blood-bowls of Aetna for the axe ’ s edge . [*] Now when that hell-cook , god-detested , had everything quite ready , he caught up a pair of my companions and proceeded deliberately to cut the throat of one of them over the yawning brazen pot ;

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: τὸν δ ’ αὖ , τένοντος ἁρπάσας ἄκρου ποδός ,
sections.translation: but the other he clutched by the tendon of his heel , and , striking him against a sharp point of rocky stone , dashed out his brains ; then , after hacking the fleshy parts with glutton cleaver , he set to grilling them , but the limbs he threw into his cauldron to seethe .

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: ἐγὼ δ ’ ὁ τλήμων δάκρυ ’ ἀπ ’ ὀφθαλμῶν χέων
sections.translation: And I , poor wretch , drew near with streaming eyes and waited on the Cyclops ; but the others kept cowering like frightened birds in crannies of the rock , and the blood forsook their skin .

sections.label: Section 409
sections.original: ἐπεὶ δ ’ ἑταίρων τῶν ἐμῶν πλησθεὶς βορᾶς
sections.translation: Anon , when he had gorged himself upon my comrades ’ flesh and

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: ἀνέπεσε , φάρυγος αἰθέρ ’ ἐξιεὶς βαρύν ,
sections.translation: had fallen on his back , breathing heavily , there came a sudden inspiration to me . I filled a cup of this Maronian wine and offered him a draught , saying , Cyclops , son of Ocean ’ s god , see here what heavenly drink the grapes of Hellas yield ,

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: θεῖον κομίζει πῶμα , Διονύσου γάνος .
sections.translation: glad gift of Dionysus . He , glutted with his shameless meal , took and drained it at one draught , and , lifting up his hand , he thanked me thus , Dearest to me of all my guests ! fair the drink thou givest me to crown so fair a feast .

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: ἡσθέντα δ ’ αὐτὸν ὡς ἐπῃσθόμην ἐγώ ,
sections.translation: Now when I saw his delight , I gave him another cup , knowing the wine would make him rue it , and he would soon be paying the penalty . Then he set to singing ; but I kept filling bumper after bumper and heating him with drink .

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: ᾄδεῖ δὲ παρὰ κλαίουσι συνναύταις ἐμοῖς
sections.translation: So there he is singing discordantly amid the weeping of my fellow-sailors , and the cave re-echoes ; but I have made my way out quietly and would fain save thee and myself , if thou wilt . Tell me then , is it your wish , or is it not , to fly from this unsocial wretch

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: ναίειν μέλαθρα Δαναΐδων νυμφῶν μέτα .
sections.translation: and take up your abode with Naiad nymphs in the halls of the Bacchic god ? Thy father within approves this scheme ; but there ! he is powerless , getting all he can out of his liquor ; his wings are snared by the cup as if he had flown against bird-lime , and he is fuddled ; but thou art young and lusty ;

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: σώθητι μετ ’ ἐμοῦ καὶ τὸν ἀρχαῖον φίλον
sections.translation: so save thyself with my help and regain thy old friend Dionysus , so little like the Cyclops .

sections.label: Section 437
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατ ’, εἰ γὰρ τήνδ ’ ἴδοιμεν ἡμέραν ,
sections.translation: Best of friends , would we might see that day , escaping the godless Cyclops ! for ’ tis long

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: χηρεύομεν . — τὸν δ ’ οὐκ ἔχομεν κατ ’ αὗ φαγεῖν .
sections.translation: we have been without the joys of men , unable to escape him . [*]

sections.label: Section 441
sections.original: ἄκουε δή νυν ἣν ἔχω τιμωρίαν
sections.translation: Hear then how I will requite this vile monster and rescue you from thraldom .

sections.label: Section 443
sections.original: λέγ ’, ὡς Ἀσιάδος οὐκ ἂν ἥδιον ψόφον
sections.translation: Tell me how ; no note of Asiatic lyre would sound more sweetly in our ears than news of the Cyclops ’ death .

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: ἐπὶ κῶμον ἕρπειν πρὸς κασιγνήτους θέλει
sections.translation: Delighted with this liquor of the Bacchic god , he fain would go a-revelling with his brethren .

sections.label: Section 447
sections.original: ξυνῆκ ’· ἔρημον ξυλλαβὼν δρυμοῖσί νιν
sections.translation: I understand ; thy purpose is to seize and slay him in the thickets when alone , or push him down a precipice .

sections.label: Section 449
sections.original: οὐδὲν τοιοῦτον · δόλιος ἡ ’ πιθυμία .
sections.translation: Not at ail ; my plan is fraught with subtlety .

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: πῶς δαί ; σοφόν τοί σ ’ ὄντ ’ ἀκούομεν πάλαι .
sections.translation: What then ? Truly we have long heard of thy cleverness .

sections.label: Section 451
sections.original: κώμου μὲν αὐτὸν τοῦδ ’ ἀπαλλάξαι , λέγων
sections.translation: I mean to keep him from this revel , saying he must not give this drink to his brethren but keep it for himself alone and lead a happy life . Then when he falls asleep , o ’ ermastered by the Bacchic god ,

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: ἀκρεμὼν ἐλαίας ἔστιν ἐν δόμοισί τις ,
sections.translation: I will put a point with this sword of mine to an olive-branch I saw lying in the cave , and will set it on fire ; and when I see it well alight , I will lift the heated brand , and , thrusting it full in the Cyclops ’ eye , melt out his sight with its blaze ;

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: ναυπηγίαν δ ’ ὡσεί τις ἁρμόζων ἀνὴρ
sections.translation: and , as when a man in fitting the timbers of a ship makes his auger spin to and fro with a double strap , so will I make the brand revolve in the eye that gives the Cyclops light and will scorch up the pupil thereof .

sections.label: Section 464
sections.original: ἰοὺ ἰού ,
sections.translation: Ho ! ho ! how glad I feel ! wild with joy at the contrivance !

sections.label: Section 466
sections.original: κἄπειτα καὶ σὲ καὶ φίλους γέροντά τε
sections.translation: That done , I will embark thee and those thou lovest with old Silenus in the deep hold of my black ship , my ship with double banks of oars , and carry you away from this land .

sections.label: Section 469
sections.original: ἔστ ’ οὖν ὅπως ἂν ὡσπερεὶ σπονδῆς θεοῦ
sections.translation: Well , can I too lay hold of the blinding brand , as though the god ’ s libation had been poured ? for I would fain have a share in this offering of blood .

sections.label: Section 472
sections.original: δεῖ γοῦν · μέγας γὰρ δαλός · οὗ ξυλληπτέον .
sections.translation: Indeed thou must , for the brand is large , and thou must help hold it .

sections.label: Section 473
sections.original: ὡς κἂν ἁμαξῶν ἑκατὸν ἀραίμην βάρος ,
sections.translation: How lightly would I lift the load of e ’ en a hundred wains , if that will help us to grub out

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: ὀφθαλμὸν ὥσπερ σφηκιὰν ἐκθύψομεν .
sections.translation: the eye of the doomed Cyclops , like a wasp ’ s nest .

sections.label: Section 476
sections.original: σιγᾶτε νῦν · δόλον γὰρ ἐξεπίστασαι ·
sections.translation: Hush ! for now thou knowest my plot in fall , and when I bid you , obey the author of it ; for I am not the man to desert my friends inside the cave and save myself alone .

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: καίτοι φύγοιμ ’ ἄν , κἀκβέβηκ ’ ἄντρου μυχῶν ·
sections.translation: And yet I might escape ; I am clear of the cavern ’ s depths already ; but no ! to desert the friends with whom I journeyed hither and only save myself is not a righteous course . [ Re-enters the cave .

sections.label: Section 483
sections.original: ἄγε , τίς πρῶτος , τίς δ ’ ἐπὶ πρώτῳ
sections.translation: Come , who will be the first and who the next to him upon the list to grip the handle of the brand ,

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: Κύκλωπος ἔσω βλεφάρων ὤσας
sections.translation: and , thrusting it into the Cyclops ’ eye , gouge out the light thereof ?

sections.label: Section 487
sections.original: σίγα σίγα . καὶ δὴ μεθύων
sections.translation: Hush ! hush ! Behold the drunkard leaves his rocky home , trolling loud some hideous lay ,

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: σκαιὸς ἀπῳδὸς καὶ κλαυσόμενος
sections.translation: a clumsy tuneless clown , whom tears await . Come , let us give this boor a lesson in revelry . Ere long will he be blind at any rate .

sections.label: Section 1.495
sections.original: μάκαρ ὅστις εὐιάζει
sections.translation: Happy he who plays the Bacchanal amid the precious streams distilled from grapes , stretched at full length for a revel , his arm around the friend he loves ,

sections.label: Section 1.500
sections.original: χλιδανῆς ἔχων ἑταίρας
sections.translation: and some fair dainty damsel on his couch , his hair perfumed with nard and glossy , the while he calls , Oh ! who will ope the door for me ?

sections.label: Section 2.503
sections.original: παπαπᾶ · πλέως μὲν οἴνου ,
sections.translation: Ha ! ha ! full of wine and merry with the feast ’ s good cheer [*] am I ,

sections.label: Section 2.505
sections.original: σκάφος ὁλκὰς ὣς γεμισθεὶς
sections.translation: my hold freighted like a merchant-ship up to my belly ’ s very top . This turf graciously invites me to seek my brother Cyclopes for a revel in the spring-tide .

sections.label: Section 2.510
sections.original: φέρε μοι , ξεῖνε , φέρ ’, ἀσκὸν ἔνδος μοι .
sections.translation: Come , stranger , bring the wine-skin hither and hand it over to me .

sections.label: Section 3.511
sections.original: καλὸν ὄμμασιν δεδορκὼς
sections.translation: Forth from the house its fair lord comes , casting his fair glance round him . We have some one to befriend us . [*] A hostile brand is awaiting thee ,

sections.label: Section 3.515
sections.original: χρόα χὡς τέρεινα νύμφα
sections.translation: no tender bride in dewy grot . No single colour will those garlands have , that soon shall cling so close about thy brow .

sections.label: Section 519
sections.original: Κύκλωψ , ἄκουσον · ὡς ἐγὼ τοῦ Βακχίου
sections.translation: ( Returning with the wineskin .) Hearken , Cyclops ; for I am well versed in the ways of Bacchus ,

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: τούτου τρίβων εἴμ ’, ὃν πιεῖν ἔδωκά σοι .
sections.translation: whom I have given thee to drink .

sections.label: Section 521
sections.original: ὁ Βάκχιος δὲ τίς θεὸς νομίζεται ;
sections.translation: And who is Bacchus ? some reputed god ?

sections.label: Section 522
sections.original: μέγιστος ἀνθρώποισιν ἐς τέρψιν βίου .
sections.translation: The greatest god men know to cheer their life .

sections.label: Section 523
sections.original: ἐρυγγάνω γοῦν αὐτὸν ἡδέως ἐγώ .
sections.translation: I like his after-taste at any rate .

sections.label: Section 524
sections.original: τοιόσδ ’ ὁ δαίμων · οὐδένα βλάπτει βροτῶν .
sections.translation: This is the kind of god he is ; he harmeth no man .

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: θεὸς δ ’ ἐν ἀσκῷ πῶς γέγηθ ’ οἴκους ἔχων ;
sections.translation: But how does a god like being housed in a wine-skin ?

sections.label: Section 526
sections.original: ὅπου τιθῇ τις , ἐνθάδ ’ ἐστὶν εὐπετής .
sections.translation: Put him where one may , he is content there .

sections.label: Section 527
sections.original: οὐ τοὺς θεοὺς χρὴ σῶμ ’ ἔχειν ἐν δέρμασιν .
sections.translation: It is not right that gods should be clad in leather .

sections.label: Section 528
sections.original: τί δ ’, εἴ σε τέρπει γ ’; ἢ τὸ δέρμα σοι πικρόν ;
sections.translation: What of that , provided he please thee ? does the leather hurt thee ?

sections.label: Section 529
sections.original: μισῶ τὸν ἀσκόν · τὸ δὲ ποτὸν φιλῶ τόδε .
sections.translation: I hate the wine-skin , but the liquor we have here I love .

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: μένων νυν αὐτοῦ πῖνε κεὐθύμει , Κύκλωψ .
sections.translation: Stay , then , Cyclops ; drink and be merry .

sections.label: Section 531
sections.original: οὐ χρή μ ’ ἀδελφοῖς τοῦδε προσδοῦναι ποτοῦ ;
sections.translation: Must I not give my brethren a share in this liquor ?

sections.label: Section 532
sections.original: ἔχων γὰρ αὐτὸς τιμιώτερος φανῇ .
sections.translation: No , keep it thyself and thou wilt appear of more honour .

sections.label: Section 533
sections.original: διδοὺς δὲ τοῖς φίλοισι χρησιμώτερος .
sections.translation: Give it my friends and I shall appear of more use .

sections.label: Section 534
sections.original: πυγμὰς ὁ κῶμος λοίδορόν τ ’ ἔριν φιλεῖ .
sections.translation: Revelling is apt to end in blows , abuse , and strife .

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: μεθύω μέν , ἔμπας δ ’ οὔτις ἂν ψαύσειέ μου .
sections.translation: I may be drunk , but no man will lay hands on me for all that .

sections.label: Section 536
sections.original: ὦ τᾶν , πεπωκότ ’ ἐν δόμοισι χρὴ μένειν .
sections.translation: Better stay at home , my friend , after a carouse .

sections.label: Section 537
sections.original: ἠλίθιος ὅστις μὴ πιὼν κῶμον φιλεῖ .
sections.translation: Who loves not revelling then is but a simpleton .
